items.title: How the Philippines and Vietnam stand up to China | Mapped Out
items.author: DW News
items.content:
China's push to dominate the South China Sea is sparking tension with Vietnam and the Philippines. Their responses are very different: the Philippines are seeking US support and seem caught up in the US-China rivalry. Vietnam is quietly copying Chinaâ€™s playbook while balancing the superpowers. Which approach works better in the South China Sea?

items.title: Motion to impeach South Korean President Yoon fails as his own party boycotts vote | DW News
items.author: DW News
items.content:
A motion to impeach South Korea's President Yoon Suk Yeol has failed after lawmakers from his own party, the People Power Party, boycotted the vote. The opposition coalition filed the impeachment order in response to the president's attempt to impose martial law on Tuesday.

items.title: These Turkish women practise self-defense, as violence against women and girls increases | DW News
items.author: DW News
items.content:
Femicide, deadly violence against women and girls, is on the rise in Turkey. Aid groups say a lack of education and a social climate favorable to perpetrators is to blame. 
DW Correspondent Julia Hahn speaks to women who are learning martial arts for self-defense, saying they must take their safety into their own hands.

items.title: Syrian rebels near Homs as gov't forces appear unable to stop them | DW News
items.author: DW News
items.content:
Islamist rebels are closing in on the city of Homs after seizing Aleppo and Hama. Government forces have appeared unable to mount an effective resistance since the rebels launched their offensive last week. Taking Homs would bring them a step closer to capturing Damascus, and toppling president Assad's government.

items.title: Syria: Islamist rebels encircle government-held Damascus | DW News
items.author: DW News
items.content:
Syrian Islamist rebels are staging uprisings against longtime Syrian dictator Bashar al-Assad on multiple fronts. As the main opposition offensive in the northwest continues to move toward Damascus, opposition factions in the south are closing in on the capital, with reports that they are within 20 miles of the city. A war monitor also said that local armed Islamist groups have taken control of most of the southern Daraa province, including Daraa city. In the north, government forces have dug in to defend the city of Homs as they fight to save President Bashar al-Assad's 24-year rule. 

Chapter Breakdown
0:00 Anti-Assad rebels close in on Syrian capital
0:44 Gasia Ohanes, DW Reporter, on how Assad's internal and external allies are responding
4:23 Ossama Muhammad, Journalist in Qamishli, Syria, on Syrian rebels' advances

#damascus #assad #syrianrebels

items.title: Official ceremony for Notre Dame reopening following reconstruction | DW News
items.author: DW News
items.content:
The reopening service will take place in the presence of President Emmanuel Macron who will address numerous heads of state and government, dignitaries, patrons, and guests invited jointly by the State and the diocese, alongside representatives of all Parisian parishes, members of the cathedral chapter, and the clergy of Paris.

During the ritual of the opening of the doors, the Archbishop will strike the closed door of Notre-Dame de Paris with his crosier. The cathedral will â€śrespondâ€ť by chanting Psalm 121 three times. After being silent for years, the cathedral will resound once again with songs of praise, and on the third chant, the doors will open.

items.title: Ghana elections dominated by concerns over worst economic crisis in a generation | DW News
items.author: DW News
items.content:
Polls have closed and vote-counting is underway in Ghana's presidential and parliamentary elections. The vote is largely a contest between the current Vice President Mahamudu Bawumia of the ruling New Patriotic Party and John Dramani Mahama of the main opposition National Democratic Congress Party. But many Ghanaians feel the leading candidates offer little hope for change, as the country struggles with its worst economic crisis in a generation.

Chapter Breakdown
0:00 Polls close in Ghana general election
0:30 Worst economic crisis in a generation overshadows elections

items.title: Syrian rebel leaders now say they control Homs as they eye Damascus | DW News
items.author: DW News
items.content:
Syrian rebel leaders say they are now in full control of the strategic city of Homs after government forces withdrew. The city's capture leaves the road to Damascus open for the main rebel offensive from the north -- while opposition factions in the south are already on the capital's doorstep. 

These images on social media show Syrian protesters toppling a statue of the president's late father in the southern suburbs of Damascus. Syrian state media report that President Bashar al-Assad is still in the capital, although he hasn't been seen in days. Government soldiers have been seen fleeing across the border into Iraq.

In cities recently captured by the rebels, residents are already celebrating the end of Assad's decades-long rule.

Chapters:
0:00 Rebel leaders say they now control Homs
3:12 Aaron Zelin, Washington Institute for Near East Policy

For more news go to: http://www.dw.com/en/

items.title: Notre Dame reopened in ceremony hosting dozens of dignitaries | DW News
items.author: DW News
items.content:
A ceremony has been taking place in Paris to mark the reopening of Notre Dame Cathedral.

President Emmanuel Macron hosted dozens of world leaders and dignitaries in the French capital to celebrate the restoration. The cathedral in the heart of Paris has undergone a painstaking renovation to restore it to its former glory after a fire gutted its roof and woodwork in 2019, causing its famous spire to collapse. The main structure only narrowly avoided destruction.

items.title: Breaking: Assad flees Syria as rebel forces enter Damascus | DW News
items.author: DW News
items.content:
Syrian President Bashar al-Assad has fled the capital Damascus to an unknown destination, after rebel forces entered the city to limited resistance.

The insurgents have declared the city free from Assad's decades-long rule, less than two weeks after launching their lightning offensive in the north of the country. Reuters reports that Syrian army command has notified its officers of the fall of the regime, citing senior military officials. Syria's Prime Minister Mohammed al- Jal-ali has announced he is ready to cooperate with a peaceful transfer of power to any leadership chosen by the people.  

For more news go to: http://www.dw.com/en/

items.title: Assad flees Damascus: What to expect now in Syria | DW News
items.author: DW News
items.content:
President Bashar al-Assad has reportedly fled the capital Damascus to an unknown destination, after rebel forces entered the city, meeting little resistance. 
The insurgents have declared the city free from Assad's rule that lasted for decades, less than two weeks after launching their lightning offensive. Citing senior military officials, Reuters reports that the Syrian army command has notified its officers of the fall of the regime. Syria's Prime Minister Mohammed al- Jal-ali has announced he is ready to cooperate in a peaceful transfer of power with any new leadership chosen by the people.

items.title: Assad flees: What it means for Syria and the wider region | DW News
items.author: DW News
items.content:
In Syria, President Bashar al-Assad's regime has collapsed. Assad reportedly fled the capital Damascus to an unknown destination, after rebel forces entered the city, meeting little resistance. 
The insurgents have declared the city free from Assad's rule, which lasted 24 years. The rebels launched their offensive against the regime less than two weeks ago. Crowds have been celebrating in the capital and across Syria.
Turkey has taken more Syrian refugees than any other country -- and Syrians living in Turkey have been celebrating after rebel forces took control of Damascus and Bashar al-Assad fled the city.
In Istanbul, Syrians have been dancing, chanting and waving flags used by Syria's opposition to mark the rebels victory. Many Syrians in Turkey have said they could not return to their towns and villages while Assad remained in power. The United Nations says 3.6 million Syrians have been living in Turkey since the Syrian war broke out in 2011.

00:00 Assad flees
03:38 DW speaks with Fawaz Gerges, Professor of Middle Eastern Political and International Relations at the London School of Economics 
10:46 DW's Gasia Ohanes reports from the Studio in Berlin
18:40 DW's Dorian Jones reports from Istanbul


For more news go to: http://www.dw.com/en/

items.author: DW News
items.content:
The fall of Bashar al-Assadâ€™s regime marks a historic day for Syrians living abroad. Many Syrians living in Lebanon have expressed cautious optimism that the end of the Assad family's more than 50-year rule will make way for a better chapter. In the meantime, Lebanon has sent army reinforcements to its border with Syria. items.title: Who is the leader of Syrian Islamist group HTS, and how is the region reacting to Assad's downfall?
items.author: DW News
items.content:
The Islamist rebel group Hayat Tahrir al-Sham, or HTS, led by Abu Mohammad al-Jolani, has toppled the regime of Syriaâ€™s President Bashar al-Assad. The group insists it has renounced its ties to al-Qaeda and that it will respect pluralism and the rights of minorities. Since the downfall of Assadâ€™s regime, the Syrian President has reportedly fled the country and been granted asylum in Russia. 
Meanwhile Israel has seized a buffer zone in the Syrian-controlled area of the Golan Heights. It says this is a temporary but necessary measure since Syrian soldiers had abandoned their positions. Prime Minister Benjamin Netanyahu has said their departure means Israel's 1974 ceasefire agreement with Syria has collapsed, and Israeli forces must now take over the territory to protect against any potential threats.

Chapter Breakdown
00:00 Anas Salkhadi, HTS rebel commander, says "Syria is for everyone"
00:34 Rebels loot presidential palace in Damascus

items.title: Germany welcomes Assad's downfall, offers Syria assistance to rebuild | DW News
items.author: DW News
items.content:
German Chancellor Olaf Scholz has welcomed the fall of the Syrian Assad regime and offered Berlin's help to stabilize the war-torn country. 
Syrians in Berlin took to the streets on Sunday to celebrate, waving flags used by the Syrian opposition. Germany is home to nearly a million Syrians who fled the civil war in their home country in 2015. Most of them were granted asylum protection and given the right to live and work in Germany.

items.title: Assad's fall shakes up regional balance of power | DW News

items.title: Biden visits Angola: Can the US counter China's influence in Africa? | DW News
items.author: DW News
items.content:
Joe Biden has become the first US president to visit Angola. It's also his first state visit to Africa during his administration. 
The trip is aimed at strengthening ties between the two countries and promoting US investments. At the heart of Biden's visit is the Lobito Corridor - a new international railway, connecting the city of Lobito on Angola's Atlantic coast to Zambia and the Democratic Republic of the Congo. The aim is to ease the export of minerals. This 5-billion-dollar investment is the largest US-funded infrastructure project in Africa and some see it as Washington's attempt to counter Chinese influence on the continent.

00:00 Biden in Angola
00:44 DW speaks with journalist and commentator Redi Tlhabi in Washington DC

For more news go to: http://www.dw.com/en/

items.title: Can Syria keep running, and keep the peace, after fall of Assad? | DW News
items.author: DW News
items.content:
Russian state media says that ousted Syrian leader Bashar al-Assad has been granted asylum in Moscow after fleeing his country. His regime collapsed in a lightning rebel offensive that began less than two weeks ago. Assad's fall has been a cause for widespread celebration across the country and among the Syrian diaspora. After almost 25 years under his rule, Syrians are beginning to turn their attention towards what rebel leaders say is a 'new era' for their homeland.

00:00 Syrians celebrate fall of Assad
03:01 Ossama Muhammad reports from Qamishli, in northeastern Syria

items.title: Syria: How to find and get justice for Assad's victims? | DW News
items.author: DW News
items.content:
Russian state media say that ousted Syrian leader Bashar al-Assad has been granted asylum in Moscow after fleeing his country. His regime collapsed in a lightning rebel offensive that began less than two weeks ago. Assad's fall has been a cause for widespread celebration across the country and among the Syrian diaspora. After almost 25 years under his rule, Syrians are beginning to turn their attention towards what rebel leaders say is a 'new era' for their homeland.
As thousands of victims are released from Assad's brutal prison system, difficulty remains in finding and releasing others, many of whom are underground in coded cells. How can justice be served with Assad in Russia?

00:00 Assad in Russia
00:27 DW's Gasia Ohanes reports from the studio in Berlin

items.title: How the HTS group could impact Syria's future | DW Shorts
items.author: DW News
items.content:
Hayat Tahrir al-Sham, or HTS, has emerged as a key player in rebel forces toppling Assad's regime. The group has a militant Islamist background but has struck a more moderate tone in recent days. Middle East expert Haid Haid from the international affairs think tank Chatham House says whether HTS is willing to share powers and accept other democratic forces remains to be seen.

items.title: Romanians split over constitutional court decision on presidential election | DW News
items.author: DW News
items.content:
Romania is now heading into a period of uncertainty following the annulment of last week's presidential elections. The country's constitutional court voided the result of the first round over concerns of foreign interference. The incumbent President Klaus Iohannis will remain in his post while the courts and the government look for ways to prevent any meddling when the election is held again. DWâ€™s Jack Parrock has been speaking to people about the fallout of the ongoing political turmoil.

items.title: Germany suspends decisions on asylum applications by Syrians | DW News
items.author: DW News
items.content:
Syrians who fled the civil war in their country are spread out across the world. Germany hosts the largest Syrian community in Europe. Many Syrians are now flooding the streets of their adopted country to celebrate the sudden and stunning end to the Assad family's rule. The German government has voiced its solidarity with the diaspora â€” and announced that it's freezing all new asylum applications from Syrians.  

DW reporter Thomas Sparrow explains.

items.title: Humanitarian situation in Syria remains dire | DW News
items.author: DW News
items.content:
The fall of the Assad regime has many Syrian refugees in Turkey hoping they'll be able to return home soon. The UN's refugee agency says Turkey has registered around 3.2 million Syrians. A day after rebels seized control of Damascus, Syrian refugees are lining up at border crossings. 
UNICEF says around 90 per cent of families in Syria are living in poverty, with more than 16 million people dependent on aid.

0:00 Syrian refugees and Syrians within the country are in need of humanitarian aid

items.title: Rebels free Syrians from Saydnaya prison | DW Shorts
items.author: DW News
items.content:
Syrian rebels released people from Saydnaya prison near Damascus. Thousands of prisoners spilled out of Assad's detention system after his fall on Sunday, December 8. Families went through Saydnaya's complex in hope of finding missing loved ones. But by Monday afternoon there was no sign of more prisoners, after a rescue team searched for hidden underground cells.

items.author: DW News
items.content:

items.author: DW News
items.content:

New York police said the man was spotted at a McDonald's near the town of Altoona. He had a firearm with a silencer and fraudulent identification matching the one used by the suspect to check into a New York hostel.

items.title: How will the security situation in Syria pan out? | DW News
items.author: DW News
items.content:
Rebel leaders in Syria have begun to assert their control, closely watched by the international community. The head of the so-called joint 'Military Operations Command,' Abu Mohammed al-Jolani has dropped the nom de guerre associated with his jihadist past and is now using his real name: Ahmed al-Shara. He's trying to bolster his legitimacy by presenting a more moderate public image. Syria's parliament says it supports the people's will to build a new future. What that will look like remains unclear, but many Syrians say it can only get better. 

00:00 What's next for Syria?
03:21 Haid Haid, Chatham House
08:42 Andrew Sinclair, Center for Global Security and Stabilization
16:14 Steven Heydemann, Smith College
21:13 Joseph Bahout, Institute for Public and International Affairs
